project_overview.project_development_objective: To: (i) increase access to basic municipal services in selected residential areas of Kabul city; (ii) redesign KM's Financial Management system to support better service delivery; and (iii) enable early emergency response in the event of an eligible emergency.
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 777,380 people in 1,773 hectares of government-owned land (73% unplanned settlements, 27% planned settlements). About 65% of beneficiaries are women and children.

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is described as 'a highly aid dependent country in conflict and with extremely fragile institutions' undergoing critical security and economic transition. The document notes deteriorating security situation with specific attacks mentioned (January 17, 2014 attack in Kabul). Kabul's population has grown due to refugee returns and conflict-induced migration.

key_components.description: Upgrading of basic municipal infrastructure (US$65M) including community roads, drains, culverts, footpaths, street lighting, community parks, solid waste collection points, and water supply where feasible. Also includes rehabilitation of trunk roads and drains. Service Delivery Support (US$20M) for Sanitation and Roads Departments including equipment for septic tank emptying, road sweeping, snow clearing, GPS trackers on 667 vehicles, and international advisor support.

key_components.component_name: Component B: Redesign KM's Financial Management System
key_components.description: Financing for an International Advisor on Municipal Finance and a consulting firm to develop a fully costed, time-bound action plan for institutional reforms integrating IT solutions for revenue management, accounting, and management development.

Household Survey (US$1M) for geo-referenced data collection on service delivery to be conducted twice during project life. Drainage Studies (US$4M) including master plan for storm water drainage system for 2030+ and detailed study for Kanal Wazir Abad catchment area with options for solving flooding problems.

key_components.component_name: Component D: Project Management, Works Design and Supervision
key_components.description: Financing for KMDP senior consultants (US$2.5M), incremental operating costs for staff (US$11M), short-term consultants (US$1.5M), professional training and knowledge sharing (US$1M), and office building construction (US$1M).

Allows rapid response following adverse natural or man-made events causing major disaster through reallocation of project funds. Enables early emergency response with quick access to funding for critical goods, works, and services.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Kabul Municipality (KM) with assistance of KMDP Team established under the Kabul Urban Reconstruction Project (KURP). Team includes well-qualified engineers, procurement and contract management specialists, social and environmental specialists, and economist. Implementation under Mayor's office with coordination across KM departments and central line ministries.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Joint integrated development program with Kabul Urban Transport Efficiency Improvement (KUTEI) project under Mayor's office. Monthly coordination meetings with Afghanistan Urban Water Supply and Sewerage Corporation (AUWSSC). Community consultation through Community Consultation Manual (CCM) processes. Gozar Cooperating Shuras (GCSs) established as local-level democratic representative bodies with equal male-female participation. Nodal Complaint Volunteers identified by GCS to inform community members on grievance registration.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: National Competitive Bidding (NCB) for works up to US$5M, International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for works above US$5M. Shopping for goods/services up to US$50,000. Consultancy firms selected using QCBS, QBS, CQS, LCS, FBS, SSS methods. Individual consultants selected per Bank guidelines. Standard Bidding Documents approved by Bank used. Procurement Plan developed and updated annually. Prior review required for contracts above specified thresholds. Procurement risk mitigation includes wide publicity through website, ARDS, UNDB, national newspapers. Complaint system established per Procurement Law Article 71-72.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results Framework (Annex 1) used to monitor PDO and output indicators. Periodic Physical and Financial Progress Monitoring reports provided monthly, quarterly, and annually in agreed formats. Social and environmental monitoring reports inform on ESIA disclosure dates, community consultations, grievance reports including land acquisition and compensation issues. Household Survey conducted twice during project life to provide baseline and monitoring data. M&E costs included in Component D. Quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) provided within 45 days of quarter end. Annual audited financial statements submitted within 6 months of fiscal year close.

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall security situation in Afghanistan makes any operation highly risky due to political and economic uncertainties. Deterioration in security situation exacerbates risks, especially given dependence of construction industry on imports. Presidential elections scheduled for April 2014 with government assuming leadership for both security and development from international community by end of 2014. Aid levels expected to fall and future aid levels remain uncertain.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Experienced staff managing project. Flexible and modest expectations. Realistic assessment of implementation period accounting for public/private sector capacity limitations and political volatility. Keeping project simple with limited focused components. Establishing strong PMU with adequate autonomy and budget. Locating core Bank team in country with higher frequency implementation support missions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Kabul Municipality lacks sufficient capacity to manage project. FM assessment highlighted lack of capacity in finance department to carry out FM functions satisfactorily. Staff lack required experience and qualifications. Procurement risk assessment revealed lack of clear system of accountability, no internal manuals, no disclosure policy, no mechanism for handling complaints, and inadequate qualified staff. KM does not have adequate capacity for independent procurement management.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of dedicated KMDP Team with adequately qualified personnel experienced with IDA-supported projects. FM staff include Finance Officer qualified in Accounting and Finance plus accountant. Procurement team includes national Procurement Specialist, Procurement Assistant, and Contract Management Officer. Centralized arrangements for accounting and audit. Bank to provide training on consultancy procurement before grant effectiveness. KMDP to obtain accreditation from Procurement Policy Unit (PPU). Gradual transfer of PMU procurement staff to KM Procurement Directorate to develop capacity.

identified_risks.risk_description: KM's financial management systems have major weaknesses in budget formulation, budget execution, revenue audits, and reconciliation by sources of revenue. Budget formulation affected by lack of timely revenue information (entirely manual collection). Budget execution weak due to weak procurement systems and lack of effective market competition. KM's capital budget execution was only 37.2% in 2011-2012 despite legal requirement to allocate 55% of annual revenue to capital budget.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component B dedicated to redesigning KM's Financial Management System with International Advisor and consulting firm support. FM Manual developed and found acceptable to Bank. Monthly reconciliation of project expenditure records with MOF records. Quarterly internal audits by MoF. External audit by Auditor General with support of Audit Advisor. Designated Account (DA) at Da Afghanistan Bank operated by Special Disbursement Unit (SDU) in Treasury Department. Project Account (PA) at Da Afghanistan Bank for KMDP with 3-month advance replenished every 30 days but no later than 90 days.

identified_risks.risk_description: Governance risks related to weak institutional capacity and potential changes in key management positions and priorities in KM. Risk of new Mayor not assuming ownership of proposed work program if leadership changes during 6-year implementation period.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Governance risks being addressed on countrywide basis and followed in project. Proactive engagement with Mayor and high-level civil servants. Regular participation in Mayor's monthly donor coordination meetings. Sustained formal and informal dialogue with Mayor. If leadership changes, strategy includes proactive engagement with new Mayor with support from Bank management. Large information sharing workshops held throughout implementation. KMDP to develop short newsletter on project to keep Districts informed.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk related to delivery monitoring and sustainability of infrastructure assets. O&M responsibility for public goods lies with KM; for private goods with utilities upon payment of user charges. Sustainability of service delivery from infrastructure investment depends on strengthening KM's financial management systems and capacities, which is challenging and will take time.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component B specifically addresses redesign of KM's FM system. National Upgrading Policy developed by Ministry of Urban Development Affairs provides framework for all urban municipalities. Community ownership addressed through Community Consultation Manual processes. Beneficiary participation with special focus on gender segregation integral to design, implementation, and M&E. Community representative councils (GCSs) with half female members. Grievance Redress System (GRS) established under KURP to handle grievances. Household Survey to provide data for monitoring demand for service delivery and investment planning.

identified_risks.risk_description: Involuntary Resettlement (OP/BP 4.12) triggered as land acquisition may be needed. Physical Cultural Resources (OP/BP 4.11) triggered as historical buildings may exist in selected neighborhoods. Environmental impacts from infrastructure works including localized and temporary adverse impacts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared to guide preparation of Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMP). Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared by KM for KUTEI applies to KMDP. Relocation and resettlement not expected as improvements involve rehabilitation within existing ROW. If minor land acquisition necessary, government land will be used, compensation payments made, or land obtained through private voluntary donations with community compensation documented. Meaningful consultation with affected persons per CCM. KMDP Safeguards staff trained in handling complaints with Bank refresher training during implementation. GRS established under KURP handles grievances. Central database maintained for monitoring and analysis. Environmental Category B project with mitigation through integration of ESMPs in bidding documents. Land mine clearance requirements included in ESMFs.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk related to public sector stakeholders including Kabul Municipality administration and Afghanistan Urban Water Supply and Sewerage Corporation (AUWSSC). AUWSSC cooperation essential for water supply delivery but has limited control due to off-budget donor funding. Risk of coordination challenges between KMDP planning and water supply availability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Bank Country Office staff to convene joint meetings with KMDP Team and Kabul AUWSSC leadership. Small interventions in water distribution system identified by AUWSSC communicated to KMDP for implementation to AUWSSC specifications. Partnership approach to make water available to more people while expanding AUWSSC's customer base. Regular coordination meetings between KMDP and AUWSSC. Liaison with Districts maintained through information sharing workshops and newsletters.

identified_risks.risk_description: Design risk posed by inclusion of Household (HH) Survey in largely infrastructure project. HH survey requires high level of technical skills (economist, statistician, sectoral skills) not available in KM or KMDP. Risk of poor quality survey.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IDA assuming responsibility for survey design as part of technical assistance to KM. Bank support to continue during survey implementation and data analysis. Technical assistance provided by Bank as part of South Asia Regional Urbanization Study. Outsourcing of survey to private firms with technical oversight.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Category B - project may cause localized and temporary adverse environmental impacts
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Grievance Redress System (GRS) established under KURP handles grievances under KMDP. KMDP maintains records in central database for monitoring and analysis. Gozar Cooperating Shuras (GCSs) established at neighborhood level with Nodal Complaint Volunteers identified to inform community members on grievance registration. Mechanism worked well under KURP and continues under KMDP. Consultation process with affected persons (APs) per Community Consultation Manual (CCM) creates awareness regarding compensation payment and grievance redress mechanisms. ESMF and informative materials distributed through various meetings.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Special focus on gender segregated participation integral to design, implementation, and M&E. Community representative councils (Gozar Cooperating Shuras) established with equal male-female participation (half members female). Special efforts to ensure female participation in community representative groups. Formation of female GCSs encouraged to increase women's participation. Women's participation in community meetings and consultation processes emphasized. About 65% of beneficiaries in residential settlements are women and children. Women's mediation in dispute resolution noted as effective under KURP.
vulnerable_groups: Residents of unplanned/informal settlements (73% of project area)
Refugee returnees (over 2 million returned to urban areas since 2002, 36% to Kabul)
Displaced persons from conflict and drought-induced migration

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity considerations. Addresses conflict risks through: (i) improving service delivery and living conditions to promote socio-economic development and stability, particularly in urban areas where impacts of international withdrawal expected to be acute; (ii) generating employment through labor-intensive municipal infrastructure works (over 2 million man-days of unskilled jobs); (iii) strengthening institutional capacities in municipal governance; (iv) improving public service delivery to enhance legitimacy of government institutions; (v) inclusive urban development addressing rapid urbanization from refugee returns and conflict-induced migration; (vi) community participation and inclusion processes to build social cohesion. Project contributes to World Bank ISN pillars: building legitimacy of institutions, providing equitable service delivery, and inclusive growth and jobs.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan faces formidable development challenges as highly aid-dependent country in conflict with extremely fragile institutions. Key fragility factors: (i) Critical security and economic transition with presidential elections April 2014 and government assuming leadership from international community by end 2014; (ii) Uncertain future aid levels as aid expected to fall post-transition; (iii) Rapid urban population growth (3.7% vs 2.4% national rate) driven by refugee returns (5+ million since 2002, 46% to urban areas, 36% to Kabul) and conflict/drought-induced migration; (iv) Kabul population doubled since 2004 to over 4 million, originally planned for <1 million; (v) About 70% of Kabul population in informal/unplanned settlements without basic services; (vi) Weak municipal governance and financial management capacity; (vii) High poverty (36% below poverty line, 50%+ vulnerable); (viii) Lowest per capita GDP in Asia; (ix) Deteriorating security situation during project preparation (January 2014 attack in Kabul); (x) Construction and services sectors (64% urban employment) expected most affected by aid decline.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Overall security situation makes any operation in Afghanistan highly risky. Specific security considerations: (i) Deterioration in security situation exacerbates risks, especially given construction industry dependence on imports; (ii) Land mine clearance requirements included in Environmental and Social Management Frameworks; (iii) Project implementation and supervision difficulties due to security constraints; (iv) Dependence on imports creates vulnerability to supply chain disruptions; (v) Realistic assessment of implementation period required accounting for security volatility; (vi) Flexible project design with modest expectations to accommodate security uncertainties; (vii) Strong PMU with adequate autonomy needed to manage security-related implementation challenges; (viii) Bank team located in-country with higher frequency implementation support missions to manage security risks.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Displacement and refugee context highly relevant: (i) Over 5 million refugees returned to Afghanistan since 2002, increasing population by ~20% largely in central, eastern, and northeastern regions; (ii) Over 2 million refugees (46% of returnee population) returned to urban areas since 2002; (iii) 36% of refugee returnees went to Kabul, significantly contributing to population growth from ~2 million (2004) to 4+ million (2012); (iv) Rapid urbanization driven by refugee returns, conflict, and drought-induced migration; (v) Informal settlements housing 70% of Kabul population largely result from refugee returns and migration; (vi) Project addresses needs of displaced/refugee populations through basic service provision in unplanned settlements; (vii) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared to address any land acquisition needs; (viii) Relocation and resettlement not expected as improvements involve rehabilitation within existing ROW; (ix) If minor land acquisition necessary, government land used or voluntary donations obtained with compensation documented; (x) Meaningful consultation with affected persons per Community Consultation Manual.
